serving.natural_language_portion: One typical piece of Huat Kueh weighs approximately 60 g, representing a single steamed cake portion as commonly served at breakfast or as a snack in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Huat Kueh — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Huat Kueh (brown spongy cake with brown sugar, steamed). All per-100g values preserved from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Steamed rice cake / brown sugar steamed cake — analogous GI estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for steamed rice-flour cakes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Huat Kueh exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 (medium) is estimated by analogy to steamed rice-flour cakes with added sugar, which cluster in the GI 60–70 range in the Sydney database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 35.94) / 100 ≈ 23.4 for a 60 g portion (35.94 g carbohydrate). Input record GI confidence was 0.667, consistent with an analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (33.8 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.7 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention, compounding the moderate sodium load of 196 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Huat Kueh due to its very high sugar content (33.8 g/100 g), which promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and raises blood pressure independently of dietary sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 33.8 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; carbohydrates 59.9 g/100 g with only 1.7 g dietary fibre/100 g produces a very high glycaemic load (GL ≈ 23 per 60 g serving).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Huat Kueh is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the sugar content of 33.8 g/100 g far exceeds safe glycaemic thresholds and the high glycaemic load of approximately 23 per serving will cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content (33.8 g/100 g) raises triglycerides and promotes de novo lipogenesis; low saturated fat (≈0.4 g/100 g) and absence of trans fat are mitigating factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Huat Kueh due to its very high free sugar content (33.8 g/100 g), which elevates serum triglycerides and contributes to cardiometabolic risk, despite the food's low fat content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar (33.8 g/100 g) and refined carbohydrate load (59.9 g/100 g) with minimal fibre (1.7 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity relevant to RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Huat Kueh, particularly during flares, as its very high sugar and refined carbohydrate content promotes inflammatory mediators including TNF-alpha and IL-6.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Huat Kueh (发糕 / 發糕, also known as Fa Gao or Prosperity Cake) is a traditional steamed cake popular in Singapore and across Chinese communities in Southeast Asia. The name 'Huat' derives from the Hokkien word for prosperity or good fortune, and the cake is traditionally associated with Chinese New Year and other auspicious occasions, though it is widely available year-round as a breakfast item or snack. The cake is made from rice flour or plain wheat flour, brown sugar, water, and a leavening agent such as baking powder or yeast. The batter is poured into small cup-shaped moulds and steamed until the top splits open in a characteristic flower-like bloom — a split top is considered auspicious, symbolising prosperity and good luck. The texture is soft, spongy, and slightly chewy, with a moist crumb. The flavour is distinctly sweet with a caramel-like depth from the brown sugar. Per 100 g, Huat Kueh provides 272 kcal, 5.1 g protein, 1.4 g fat, 59.9 g carbohydrates, 33.8 g sugar, 1.7 g dietary fibre, and 196 mg sodium, sourced from the HPB Singapore Foodo database. Clinically, Huat Kueh is a high-concern food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes (sugar 33.8 g/100 g, GL ≈ 23 per serving), hypertension (high sugar drives insulin-mediated sodium retention), and cardiovascular disease (elevated triglyceride risk from free sugar). It is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable in standard preparations.

How many calories in Huat Kueh?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 for an exact match to Huat Kueh (brown spongy cake with brown sugar, steamed). GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.667) from the University of Sydney GI Database, consistent with the input record.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Huat Kueh as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat estimated at 0.4 g/100 g based on total fat of 1.4 g/100 g and typical fat profile of rice-flour steamed cakes (predominantly saturated from minor coconut or palm oil traces); this is an approximation and should be confirmed if a more detailed HPB or USDA entry becomes available. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (steamed rice-flour cakes with added sugar); no direct GI measurement for Huat Kueh is available. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (65 × 35.94 g carbohydrate per 60 g serving) / 100 ≈ 23.4. Food is classified as a Singaporean Chinese seasonal/festive item; primary cuisine tagged as Singaporean Chinese reflecting its Chinese cultural origin within the Singapore food context.
